documentation: Azimuth angle of propagation.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.source.ModeSource._dir_vector
documentation: Source direction normal vector in cartesian coordinates.

Injects current source to excite modal profile on finite extent plane.

# Example

```pycon
>>> pulse = GaussianPulse(freq0=200e12, fwidth=20e12)
>>> mode_spec = ModeSpec(target_neff=2.)
>>> mode_source = ModeSource(
...     size=(10,10,0),
...     source_time=pulse,
...     mode_spec=mode_spec,
...     mode_index=1,
...     direction='-')
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.source.PlaneWave
documentation:
Uniform current distribution on an infinite extent plane. One element of size must be zero.

# Example

```pycon
>>> pulse = GaussianPulse(freq0=200e12, fwidth=20e12)
>>> pw_source = PlaneWave(size=(inf,0,inf), source_time=pulse, pol_angle=0.1, direction='+')
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.source.GaussianBeam
documentation:
Guassian distribution on finite extent plane.

# Example

```pycon
>>> pulse = GaussianPulse(freq0=200e12, fwidth=20e12)
>>> gauss = GaussianBeam(
...     size=(0,3,3),
...     source_time=pulse,
...     pol_angle=np.pi / 2,
...     direction='+',
...     waist_radius=1.0)
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.source.AstigmaticGaussianBeam
documentation:
This class implements the simple astigmatic Gaussian beam described in Kochkina et al.,
Applied Optics, vol. 52, issue 24, 2013. The simple astigmatic Guassian distribution allows
both an elliptical intensity profile and different waist locations for the two principal axes
of the ellipse. When equal waist sizes and equal waist distances are specified in the two
directions, this source becomes equivalent to {class}`GaussianBeam`.

# Example

```pycon
>>> pulse = GaussianPulse(freq0=200e12, fwidth=20e12)
>>> gauss = AstigmaticGaussianBeam(
...     size=(0,3,3),
...     source_time=pulse,
...     pol_angle=np.pi / 2,
...     direction='+',
...     waist_sizes=(1.0, 2.0),
...     waist_distances = (3.0, 4.0))
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.simulation.Simulation.plot_eps

The permittivity is plotted in grayscale based on its value at the specified frequency.

: Frequency to evaluate the relative permittivity of all mediums.
  If not specified, evaluates at infinite frequency.

monitor_alpha

documentation:
Compute the smallest Box restricted to the first quadrant in the presence of symmetries
that fully covers the original Box when symmetries are applied.

: Rectangular geometry.

: The smallest Box such that any point in `box` is either in `new_box` or can be
  mapped from `new_box` using the simulation symmetries.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.simulation.Simulation.discretize
documentation:
Grid containing only cells that intersect with a {class}`Box`.

# Parameters

box

: Rectangular geometry within simulation to discretize.

snap_zero_dim

: If `True`, and the `box` has size zero along a given direction, the `grid` is
  defined to also have a zero-sized cell exactly centered at the `box` center. If
  false, the `simulation` grid cell containing the `box` center is instead used.

kwargs :

: Extra keyword arguments passed to `discretize_inds` method of {class}`.Grid`.

# Returns

{class}`Grid`

: The FDTD subgrid containing simulation points that intersect with `box`.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.simulation.Simulation.epsilon
documentation:
Get array of permittivity at volume specified by box and freq.

# Parameters

box

: Rectangular geometry specifying where to measure the permittivity.

coord_key

: Specifies at what part of the grid to return the permittivity at.
  Accepted values are `{'centers', 'boundaries', 'Ex', 'Ey', 'Ez'}`.
  The field values (eg. 'Ex') correspond to the correponding field locations on the yee
  lattice. If field values are selected, the corresponding epsilon component from the
  main diagonal of the epsilon tensor is returned. Otherwise, the average of the diagonal
  values is returned.

freq

: The frequency to evaluate the mediums at.
  If not specified, evaluates at infinite frequency.

# Returns

xarray.DataArray

: Datastructure containing the relative permittivity values and location coordinates.
  For details on xarray DataArray objects,
  refer to [xarray's Documentaton](https://tinyurl.com/2zrzsp7b).

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.simulation.Simulation.epsilon_on_grid
documentation:
Get array of permittivity at a given freq on a given grid.

# Parameters

grid

: Grid specifying where to measure the permittivity.

coord_key

: Specifies at what part of the grid to return the permittivity at.
  Accepted values are `{'centers', 'boundaries', 'Ex', 'Ey', 'Ez'}`.
  The field values (eg. 'Ex') correspond to the correponding field locations on the yee
  lattice. If field values are selected, the corresponding epsilon component from the
  main diagonal of the epsilon tensor is returned. Otherwise, the average of the diagonal
  values is returned.

freq

: The frequency to evaluate the mediums at.
  If not specified, evaluates at infinite frequency.

# Returns

xarray.DataArray

: Datastructure containing the relative permittivity values and location coordinates.
  For details on xarray DataArray objects,
  refer to [xarray's Documentaton](https://tinyurl.com/2zrzsp7b).

class_variables.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.simulation.Simulation.version
documentation: Validating setup

Contains all information about Tidy3d simulation.

# Example

```pycon
>>> from tidy3d import Sphere, Cylinder, PolySlab
>>> from tidy3d import UniformCurrentSource, GaussianPulse
>>> from tidy3d import FieldMonitor, FluxMonitor
>>> from tidy3d import GridSpec, AutoGrid
>>> from tidy3d import BoundarySpec, Boundary
>>> sim = Simulation(
...     size=(2.0, 2.0, 2.0),
...     grid_spec=GridSpec(
...         grid_x = AutoGrid(min_steps_per_wvl = 20),
...         grid_y = AutoGrid(min_steps_per_wvl = 20),
...         grid_z = AutoGrid(min_steps_per_wvl = 20)
...     ),
...     run_time=40e-11,
...     structures=[
...         Structure(
...             geometry=Box(size=(1, 1, 1), center=(-1, 0, 0)),
...             medium=Medium(permittivity=2.0),
...         ),
...     ],
...     sources=[
...         UniformCurrentSource(
...             size=(0, 0, 0),
...             center=(0, 0.5, 0),
...             polarization="Hx",
...             source_time=GaussianPulse(
...                 freq0=2e14,
...                 fwidth=4e13,
...             ),
...         )
...     ],
...     monitors=[
...         FieldMonitor(size=(0, 0, 0), center=(0, 0, 0), freqs=[1.5e14, 2e14], name='point'),
...         FluxMonitor(size=(1, 1, 0), center=(0, 0, 0), freqs=[2e14, 2.5e14], name='flux'),
...     ],
...     symmetry=(0, 0, 0),
...     boundary_spec=BoundarySpec(
...         x = Boundary.pml(num_layers=20),
...         y = Boundary.pml(num_layers=30),
...         z = Boundary.periodic(),
...     ),
...     shutoff=1e-6,
...     courant=0.8,
...     subpixel=False,
... )
```

documentation: Return a list of the three Coord1D objects as numpy arrays.

Holds data about a set of x,y,z positions on a grid.

# Example

```pycon
>>> x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 10)
>>> y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 11)
>>> z = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12)
>>> coords = Coords(x=x, y=y, z=z)
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.FieldGrid
documentation:
Holds the grid data for a single field.

# Example

```pycon
>>> x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 10)
>>> y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 11)
>>> z = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12)
>>> coords = Coords(x=x, y=y, z=z)
>>> field_grid = FieldGrid(x=coords, y=coords, z=coords)
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.YeeGrid

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.YeeGrid.grid_dict
documentation: The Yee grid coordinates associated to various field components as a dictionary.

Holds the yee grid coordinates for each of the E and H positions.

# Example

```pycon
>>> x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 10)
>>> y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 11)
>>> z = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12)
>>> coords = Coords(x=x, y=y, z=z)
>>> field_grid = FieldGrid(x=coords, y=coords, z=coords)
>>> yee_grid = YeeGrid(E=field_grid, H=field_grid)
>>> Ex_coords = yee_grid.E.x
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.Grid.yee
documentation:
Return the {class}`YeeGrid` defining the yee cell locations for this {class}`Grid`.

# Returns

{class}`YeeGrid`

: Stores coordinates of all of the components on the yee lattice.

# Example

```pycon
>>> x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 10)
>>> y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 11)
>>> z = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12)
>>> coords = Coords(x=x, y=y, z=z)
>>> grid = Grid(boundaries=coords)
>>> yee_cells = grid.yee
>>> Ex_positions = yee_cells.E.x
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.Grid.discretize_inds
documentation:
Start and stopping indexes for the cells that intersect with a {class}`Box`.

# Parameters

box

: Rectangular geometry within simulation to discretize.

extend

: If `True`, ensure that the returned indexes extend sufficiently in very direction to
  be able to interpolate any field component at any point within the `box`.

extend_2d_normal

: If `True`, and the box is size zero along a single dimension, ensure that the returned
  indexes extend sufficiently along that dimension to be able to interpolate to the
  box center from data that lives on grid cell centers.

# Returns

List\[Tuple[int, int]\]

: The (start, stop) indexes of the cells that intersect with `box` in each of the three
  dimensions.
pylint:disable=too-many-locals

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid.Grid.periodic_subspace
documentation:
Pick a subspace of 1D boundaries within `range(ind_beg, ind_end)`. If any indexes lie
outside of the grid boundaries array, periodic padding is used, where the zeroth and last
element of the boundaries are identified.

# Parameters

axis

: Axis index along which to pick the subspace.

ind_beg

: Starting index for the subspace.

ind_end

: Ending index for the subspace.

# Returns

Coords1D

: The subspace of the grid along `axis`.

Contains all information about the spatial positions of the FDTD grid.

# Example

```pycon
>>> x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 10)
>>> y = np.linspace(-1, 1, 11)
>>> z = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12)
>>> coords = Coords(x=x, y=y, z=z)
>>> grid = Grid(boundaries=coords)
>>> centers = grid.centers
>>> sizes = grid.sizes
>>> yee_grid = grid.yee
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.parse_structures
documentation:
Calculate the positions of all bounding box interfaces along a given axis.
In this implementation, in most cases the complexity should be O(len(structures)\*\*2),
although the worst-case complexity may approach O(len(structures)\*\*3).
However this should only happen in some very contrived cases.

# Parameters

axis

: Axis index along which to operate.

structures

: List of structures, with the simulation structure being the first item.

wavelength

: Wavelength to use for the step size and for dispersive media epsilon.

min_steps_per_wvl

: Minimum requested steps per wavelength.

dl_min: pd.NonNegativeFloat

: Lower bound of grid size.

# Returns

interval_coords

: An array of coordinates, where the first element is the simulation min boundary, the
  last element is the simulation max boundary, and the intermediate coordinates are all
  locations where a structure has a bounding box edge along the specified axis.
  The boundaries are filtered such that no interval is smaller than the smallest
  of the `max_steps`.

max_steps

: An array of size `interval_coords.size - 1` giving the maximum grid step required in
  each `interval_coords[i]:interval_coords[i+1]` interval, depending on the materials
  in that interval, the supplied wavelength, and the minimum required step per wavelength.
pylint:disable=too-many-locals, too-many-arguments

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.insert_bbox
documentation:
Figure out where to place the bounding box coordinates of current structure.
For both the left and the right bounds of the structure along the meshing direction,
we check if they are not too close to an already existing coordinate, and if the
structure is not completely covered by another structure at that location.
Only then we add that boundary to the list of interval coordinates.
We also don't add the bounds if `str_ind==0`, since the domain bounds have already
been added to the interval coords at the start.
We also compute `indmin` and `indmax` indexes into the list of intervals, such that
the current structure is added to all intervals in the range (indmin, indmax).

# Parameters

intervals

: Dictionary containing the coordinates of the interval boundaries, and a list
  of lists of structures contained in each interval.

str_ind

: Index of the current structure.

str_bbox

: Bounding box of the current structure.

bbox_contained_2d

: List of 3D bounding boxes that contain the current structure in 2D.

min_step

: Absolute minimum interval size to impose.
pylint:disable=too-many-locals,too-many-arguments

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.reorder_structures_enforced_to_end

# Parameters

Tuple\[int, List[StructureType]\]

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.make_grid_multiple_intervals
documentation:
Create grid steps in multiple connecting intervals of length specified by
`len_interval_list`. The maximal allowed step size in each interval is given by
`max_dl_list`. The maximum ratio between neighboring steps is bounded by `max_scale`.

# Parameters

max_dl_list

: Maximal allowed step size of each interval.

len_interval_list

: A list of interval lengths

max_scale

: Maximal ratio between consecutive steps.

is_periodic

: Apply periodic boundary condition or not.

# Returns

List\[ArrayLike[float, 1]\]

: A list of of step sizes in each interval.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.grid_multiple_interval_analy_refinement
documentation:
Analytical refinement for multiple intervals. "analytical" meaning we allow
non-integar step sizes, so that we don't consider snapping here.

Parameters
----------
max_dl_list : ArrayLike[float, 1]
    Maximal allowed step size of each interval.
len_interval_list : ArrayLike[float, 1]
    A list of interval lengths
max_scale : float
    Maximal ratio between consecutive steps.
is_periodic : bool
    Apply periodic boundary condition or not.

Returns
-------
Tuple[ArrayLike[float, 1], ArrayLike[float, 1]]
    left and right step sizes of each interval.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.make_grid_in_interval
documentation:
Create a set of grid steps in an interval of length `len_interval`,
with first step no larger than `max_scale * left_neighbor_dl` and last step no larger than
`max_scale * right_neighbor_dl`, with maximum ratio `max_scale` between
neighboring steps. All steps should be no larger than `max_dl`.

# Parameters

left_neighbor_dl

: Step size to left boundary of the interval.

right_neighbor_dl

: Step size to right boundary of the interval.

max_dl

: Maximal step size within the interval.

max_scale

: Maximal ratio between consecutive steps.

len_interval: float

: Length of the interval.

# Returns

ArrayLike[float, 1]

: A list of step sizes in the interval.
pylint:disable=too-many-locals, too-many-return-statements, too-many-arguments

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.mesher.GradedMesher.grid_type_in_interval
documentation:
Mesh type check (in an interval).

Parameters
----------
left_dl : float
    Step size at left boundary of the interval.
right_dl : float
    Step size at right boundary of the interval.
max_dl : float
    Maximal step size within the interval.
max_scale : float
    Maximal ratio between consecutive steps.
len_interval: float
    Length of the interval.

Returns
-------
grid_type : int
    -1 for single pixel grid
    0 for uniform grid
    1 for small to large to optionally plateau grid
    2 for small to large to optionally plateau to small grid

Implements automatic nonuniform meshing with a set minimum steps per wavelength and
a graded mesh expanding from higher- to lower-resolution regions.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.log.set_logging_level
documentation:
Set tidy3d logging level priority.

# Parameters

level

: The lowest priority level of logging messages to display.
  One of `{'debug', 'info', 'warning', 'error'}` (listed in increasing priority).

# Example

```pycon
>>> log.debug('this message should not appear (default logging level = INFO')
>>> set_logging_level('debug')
>>> log.debug('this message should appear now')
```

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.log.set_logging_file
documentation:
Set a file to write log to, independently from the stdout and stderr
output chosen using {meth}`set_logging_level`.

# Parameters

fname

: Path to file to direct the output to.

filemode

: 'w' or 'a', defining if the file should be overwritten or appended.

level

: One of 'debug', 'info', 'warning', 'error', 'critical'. This is
  set for the file independently of the console output level set by
  {meth}`set_logging_level`.

# Example

```pycon
>>> set_logging_file('tidy3d_log.log')
>>> log.warning('this warning will appear in the tidy3d_log.log')
```

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.asynchronous._run_async
documentation:
Submits a set of {class}`.Simulation` objects to server, starts running,
monitors progress, downloads, and loads results as a {class}`.BatchData` object.
Uses `asyncio` to perform these steps asynchronously.

# Parameters

simulations

: Mapping of task name to simulation.

folder_name

: Name of folder to store each task on web UI.

path_dir

: Base directory where data will be downloaded, by default current working directory.

callback_url

: Http PUT url to receive simulation finish event. The body content is a json file with
  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

num_workers: int = None

: Number of tasks to submit at once in a batch, if None, will run all at the same time.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Note

This is an experimental feature and may not work on all systems or configurations.
For more details, see `https://realpython.com/async-io-python/`.

# Returns

{class}`BatchData`

: Contains the {class}`.SimulationData` of each {class}`.Simulation` in {class}`Batch`.
pylint:disable=too-many-arguments, too-many-locals

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.asynchronous.run_async

  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

num_workers: int = None

: Number of tasks to submit at once in a batch, if None, will run all at the same time.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Note

This is an experimental feature and may not work on all systems or configurations.
For more details, see `https://realpython.com/async-io-python/`.

# Returns

{class}`BatchData`

: Contains the {class}`.SimulationData` of each {class}`.Simulation` in {class}`Batch`.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec1d.make_coords
documentation:
Generate 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries, based on simulation parameters.
Symmetry, and PML layers will be treated here.

# Parameters

axis

: Axis of this direction.

structures

: List of structures present in simulation, the first one being the simulation domain.

symmetry

: Reflection symmetry across a plane bisecting the simulation domain
  normal to each of the three axes.

wavelength

: Free-space wavelength.

num_pml_layers

: number of layers in the absorber + and - direction along one dimension.

# Returns

{class}`.Coords1D`:

: 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec1d._make_coords_initial
documentation:
Generate 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries, based on simulation parameters.
Symmetry, PML etc. are not considered in this method.

For auto nonuniform generation, it will take some more arguments.

# Parameters

structures

: List of structures present in simulation, the first one being the simulation domain.

kwargs

: Other arguments

# Returns

{class}`.Coords1D`:

: 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec1d._add_pml_to_bounds
documentation:
Append absorber layers to the beginning and end of the simulation bounds
along one dimension.

Parameters
----------
num_layers : Tuple[int, int]
    number of layers in the absorber + and - direction along one dimension.
bound_coords : np.ndarray
    coordinates specifying boundaries between cells along one dimension.

Returns
-------
np.ndarray
    New bound coordinates along dimension taking abosrber into account.
Abstract base class, defines 1D grid generation specifications.

{class}`.Coords1D`:

: 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries.

Custom 1D grid supplied as a list of grid cell sizes centered on the simulation center.

# Example

```pycon
>>> grid_1d = CustomGrid(dl=[0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.2, 0.2])
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.AutoGrid

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.AutoGrid._make_coords_initial
documentation:
Customized 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries.

# Parameters

axis

: Axis of this direction.

structures

: List of structures present in simulation.

wavelength

: Free-space wavelength.

symmetry

: Reflection symmetry across a plane bisecting the simulation domain
  normal to each of the three axes.

is_periodic

: Apply periodic boundary condition or not.

# Returns

{class}`.Coords1D`:

: 1D coords to be used as grid boundaries.

Specification for non-uniform grid along a given dimension.

# Example

```pycon
>>> grid_1d = AutoGrid(min_steps_per_wvl=16, max_scale=1.4)
```

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.auto_grid_used
documentation: True if any of the three dimensions uses {class}`.AutoGrid`.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.wavelength_from_sources
documentation:
Define a wavelength based on supplied sources. Called if auto mesh is used and
`self.wavelength is None`.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.override_structures_used
documentation:
Along each axis, `True` if any override structure is used. However,
it is still `False` if only {class}`.MeshOverrideStructure` is supplied, and
their `dl[axis]` all take the `None` value.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.make_grid
documentation:
Make the entire simulation grid based on some simulation parameters.

Parameters
----------
structures : List[Structure]
    List of structures present in the simulation. The first structure must be the
    simulation geometry with the simulation background medium.
symmetry : Tuple[Symmetry, Symmetry, Symmetry]
    Reflection symmetry across a plane bisecting the simulation domain
    normal to each of the three axes.
sources : List[SourceType]
    List of sources.
num_pml_layers : List[Tuple[float, float]]
    List containing the number of absorber layers in - and + boundaries.

Returns
-------
Grid:
    Entire simulation grid.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.auto
documentation:
Use the same {class}`AutoGrid` along each of the three directions.

# Parameters

wavelength

: Free-space wavelength for automatic nonuniform grid. It can be 'None'
  if there is at least one source in the simulation, in which case it is defined by
  the source central frequency.

min_steps_per_wvl

: Minimal number of steps per wavelength in each medium.

max_scale

: Sets the maximum ratio between any two consecutive grid steps.

override_structures

: A list of structures that is added on top of the simulation structures in
  the process of generating the grid. This can be used to refine the grid or make it
  coarser depending than the expected need for higher/lower resolution regions.

dl_min: pd.NonNegativeFloat

: Lower bound of grid size.

mesher

: The type of mesher to use to generate the grid automatically.

# Returns

GridSpec

: {class}`GridSpec` with the same automatic nonuniform grid settings in each direction.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.grid.grid_spec.GridSpec.uniform
documentation:
Use the same {class}`UniformGrid` along each of the three directions.

# Parameters

dl

: Grid size for uniform grid generation.

# Returns

GridSpec

: {class}`GridSpec` with the same uniform grid size in each direction.

Collective grid specification for all three dimensions.

# Example

```pycon
>>> uniform = UniformGrid(dl=0.1)
>>> custom = CustomGrid(dl=[0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.2, 0.2])
>>> auto = AutoGrid(min_steps_per_wvl=12)
>>> grid_spec = GridSpec(grid_x=uniform, grid_y=custom, grid_z=auto, wavelength=1.5)
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.Geometry.inside
documentation:
For input arrays `x`, `y`, `z` of arbitrary but identical shape, return an array
with the same shape which is `True` for every point in zip(x, y, z) that is inside the
volume of the {class}`Geometry`, and `False` otherwise.

# Parameters

x

: Array of point positions in x direction.

y

: Array of point positions in y direction.

z

: Array of point positions in z direction.

# Returns

np.ndarray[bool]

: `True` for every point that is inside the geometry.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.Box.surfaces
documentation:
Returns a list of 6 {class}`Box` instances corresponding to each surface of a 3D volume.
The output surfaces are stored in the order [x-, x+, y-, y+, z-, z+], where x, y, and z
denote which axis is perpendicular to that surface, while "-" and "+" denote the direction
of the normal vector of that surface. If a name is provided, each output surface's name
will be that of the provided name appended with the above symbols. E.g., if the provided
name is "box", the x+ surfaces's name will be "box_x+".

# Parameters

size

: Size of object in x, y, and z directions.

center

: Center of object in x, y, and z.

# Example

```pycon
>>> b = Box.surfaces(size=(1, 2, 3), center=(3, 2, 1))
```

documentation:
Map a point (x,y) from local to global coordinate system in the
side cross section.

The definition of the local: y=0 lies at the base if `sidewall_angle>=0`,
and at the top if `sidewall_angle<0`; x=0 aligns with the corresponding
`self.center`.

axis

: Integer index into 'xyz' (0, 1, 2).

coords

: The value in the planar coordinate.

# Returns

Tuple[float, float]

: The point in the global coordinate for plotting `_intersection_side`.

conical frustum or a cone.

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab.correct_shape
documentation:
Makes sure vertices size is correct.
Make sure no intersecting edges.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab.no_complex_self_intersecting_polygon_at_reference_plane
SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab.no_self_intersecting_polygon_during_extrusion
documentation:
In this simple polyslab, we don't support self-intersecting polygons yet, meaning that
any normal cross section of the PolySlab cannot be self-intersecting. This part checks
if any self-interction will occur during extrusion with non-zero sidewall angle.

There are two types of self-intersection, known as edge events,
that can occur during dilation:
1\) neighboring vertex-vertex crossing. This type of edge event can be treated with
`ComplexPolySlab` which divides the polyslab into a list of simple polyslabs.

2\) other types of edge events that can create holes/islands or split polygons.
To detect this, we sample \_N_SAMPLE_POLYGON_INTERSECT cross sections to see if any creation
of polygons/holes, and changes in vertices number.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab.from_gds
documentation:
Import {class}`PolySlab` from a `gdstk.Cell` or a `gdspy.Cell`.

# Parameters

gds_cell

: `gdstk.Cell` or `gdspy.Cell` containing 2D geometric data.

axis

: Integer index into the polygon's slab axis. (0,1,2) -> (x,y,z).

slab_bounds: Tuple[float, float]

: Minimum and maximum positions of the slab along `axis`.

gds_layer

: Layer index in the `gds_cell`.

gds_dtype

: Data-type index in the `gds_cell`.
  If `None`, imports all data for this layer into the returned list.

gds_scale

: Length scale used in GDS file in units of MICROMETER.
  For example, if gds file uses nanometers, set `gds_scale=1e-3`.
  Must be positive.

dilation

: Dilation of the polygon in the base by shifting each edge along its
  normal outwards direction by a distance;
  a negative value corresponds to erosion.

sidewall_angle

: Angle of the sidewall.
  `sidewall_angle=0` (default) specifies vertical wall,
  while `0<sidewall_angle<np.pi/2` for the base to be larger than the top.

reference_plane

: The position of the GDS layer. It can be at the `bottom`, `middle`,
  or `top` of the PolySlab. E.g. if `axis=1`, `bottom` refers to the
  negative side of y-axis, and `top` refers to the positive side of y-axis.

# Returns

List\[{class}`PolySlab`\]

: List of {class}`PolySlab` objects sharing `axis` and slab bound properties.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab._intersections_side
documentation:
Find shapely geometries intersecting planar geometry with axis orthogonal to slab.

For slanted polyslab, the procedure is as follows,
1\) Find out all z-coordinates where the plane will intersect directly with a vertex.
Denote the coordinates as (z_0, z_1, z_2, ... )
2\) Find out all polygons that can be formed between z_i and z\_\{i+1}. There are two
types of polygons:

> 1. formed by the plane intersecting the edges
>
> b) formed by the plane intersecting the vertices.
> For either type, one needs to compute:
>
> > 1. intersecting position
> > 2. angle between the plane and the intersecting edge
>
> For a), both are straightforward to compute; while for b), one needs to compute
> which edge the plane will slide into.

3\) Looping through z_i, and merge all polygons. The partition by z_i is because once
the plane intersects the vertex, it can intersect with other edges during
the extrusion.

# Parameters

position

: Position along `axis`.

axis

: Integer index into 'xyz' (0,1,2).

# Returns

List[shapely.geometry.base.BaseGeometry]

: List of 2D shapes that intersect plane.
  For more details refer to
  [Shapely's Documentaton](https://shapely.readthedocs.io/en/stable/project.html).

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab._edge_events_detection

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab._neighbor_vertices_crossing_detection
documentation:
Detect if neighboring vertices will cross after a dilation distance dist.

# Parameters

vertices

: Shape (N, 2) defining the polygon vertices in the xy-plane.

dist

: Distance to offset.

ignore_at_dist

: whether to ignore the event right at dist\`.

# Returns

float

: the absolute value of the maximal allowed dilation
  if there are any crossing, otherwise return `None`.

    and the shift of vertices in direction parallel to the edges.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.PolySlab._surface_area
documentation: Returns object's surface area given bounds.

Polygon extruded with optional sidewall angle along axis direction.

# Example

```pycon
>>> vertices = np.array([(0,0), (1,0), (1,1)])
>>> p = PolySlab(vertices=vertices, axis=2, slab_bounds=(-1, 1))
```

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.GeometryGroup.inside

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.components.geometry.GeometryGroup.inside_meshgrid
documentation:

x

y

np.ndarray[bool]

modules.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi
documentation: Provides lowest level, user-facing interface to server.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.run
documentation:
Submits a {class}`.Simulation` to server, starts running, monitors progress, downloads,
and loads results as a {class}`.SimulationData` object.

# Parameters

simulation

: Simulation to upload to server.

task_name

: Name of task.

path

: Path to download results file (.hdf5), including filename.

folder_name

: Name of folder to store task on web UI.

callback_url

: Http PUT url to receive simulation finish event. The body content is a json file with
  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Returns

{class}`.SimulationData`

: Object containing solver results for the supplied {class}`.Simulation`.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.upload
documentation:
Upload simulation to server, but do not start running {class}`.Simulation`.

# Parameters

simulation

: Simulation to upload to server.

task_name

: Name of task.

folder_name

: Name of folder to store task on web UI

callback_url

: Http PUT url to receive simulation finish event. The body content is a json file with
  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Returns

TaskId

: Unique identifier of task on server.

# Note

To start the simulation running, must call {meth}`start` after uploaded.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.get_info
documentation:
Return information about a task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

# Returns

{class}`TaskInfo`

: Object containing information about status, size, credits of task.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.start
documentation:
Start running the simulation associated with task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

solver_version

: Supply or override a specific solver version to the task.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Note

To monitor progress, can call {meth}`monitor` after starting simulation.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.get_run_info
documentation:
Gets the % done and field_decay for a running task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

# Returns

perc_done

: Percentage of run done (in terms of max number of time steps).
  Is `None` if run info not available.

field_decay

: Average field intensity normlized to max value (1.0).
  Is `None` if run info not available.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.monitor
documentation:
Print the real time task progress until completion.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Note

To load results when finished, may call {meth}`load`.
pylint: disable=too-many-statements, too-many-locals, too-many-branches

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.download
documentation:
Download results of task and log to file.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to .hdf5 data file (including filename).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.download_json
documentation:
Download the `.json` file associated with the {class}`.Simulation` of a given task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to .json file of simulation (including filename).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.download_hdf5
documentation:
Download the `.hdf5` file associated with the {class}`.Simulation` of a given task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to .hdf5 file of simulation (including filename).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.load_simulation
documentation:
Download the `.json` file of a task and load the associated {class}`.Simulation`.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to .json file of simulation (including filename).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Returns

{class}`.Simulation`

: Simulation loaded from downloaded json file.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.download_log
documentation:
Download the tidy3d log file associated with a task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to log file (including filename).

# Note

To load downloaded results into data, call {meth}`load` with option `replace_existing=False`.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.load
documentation:
Download and Load simultion results into {class}`.SimulationData` object.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

path

: Download path to .hdf5 data file (including filename).

replace_existing: bool = True

: Downloads the data even if path exists (overwriting the existing).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Returns

{class}`.SimulationData`

: Object containing simulation data.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.delete
documentation:
Delete server-side data associated with task.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

# Returns

TaskInfo

: Object containing information about status, size, credits of task.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.delete_old
documentation:
Delete all tasks older than a given amount of days.

Parameters
----------
folder : str
    Only allowed to delete in one folder at a time.
days_old : int = 100
    Minimum number of days since the task creation.

Returns
-------
int
    Total number of tasks deleted.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.get_tasks
documentation:
Get a list with the metadata of the last `num_tasks` tasks.

# Parameters

num_tasks

: The number of tasks to return, or, if `None`, return all.

order

: Return the tasks in order of newest-first or oldest-first.

folder: str = "default"

: Folder from which to get the tasks.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.estimate_cost
documentation:
Compute the maximum flex unit charge for a given task, assuming the simulation runs for
the full `run_time`. If early shut-off is triggered, the cost is adjusted proporionately.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi.real_cost
documentation:
Get the billed cost for given task after it has been run.

# Note

> The billed cost may not be immediately available when the task status is set to `success`,
> but should be available shortly after.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi._download_file
documentation:
Download a specific file from server.

# Parameters

task_id

: Unique identifier of task on server. Returned by {meth}`upload`.

fname

: Name of the file on server (eg. `monitor_data.hdf5`, `tidy3d.log`, `simulation.json`)

path

: Path where the file will be downloaded to (including filename).

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.webapi._rm_file
documentation: Clear path if it exists.

Tuple\[{class}`.PoleResidue`, float\]

: Best results of multiple fits: (dispersive medium, RMS error).

         ["wl",     "k"],
         [float,  float],
          .        .
          .        .
          .        .]]

Parameters
----------
data_load : List
    Loaded data from URL

Raises
------
ValidationError
    Or other exceptions

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.dispersion.fit.DispersionFitter.from_url
documentation:
loads {class}`DispersionFitter` from url linked to a csv/txt file that
contains wavelength (micron), n, and optionally k data. Preferred from
refractiveindex.info.

# Hint

The data file from url should be in this format (delimiter not displayed
here, and note that the strings such as "wl", "n" need to be included
in the file):

- For lossless media:

  ```
  wl       n
  [float] [float]
  .       .
  .       .
  .       .
  ```

- For lossy media:

  ```
  wl       n
  [float] [float]
  .       .
  .       .
  .       .
  wl       k
  [float] [float]
  .       .
  .       .
  .       .
  ```

# Parameters

url_file

: Url link to the data file.
  e.g. "<https://refractiveindex.info/data_csv.php?datafile=data/main/Ag/Johnson.yml>"

delimiter

: E.g. in refractiveindex.info, it'll be "," for csv file, and "\\t" for txt file.

ignore_k

: Ignore the k data if they are present, so the fitted material is lossless.

# Returns

{class}`DispersionFitter`

: A {class}`DispersionFitter` instance.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.dispersion.fit.DispersionFitter.from_file
documentation:
Loads {class}`DispersionFitter` from file containing wavelength, n, k data.

# Parameters

fname

: Path to file containing wavelength (um), n, k (optional) data in columns.

loadtxt_kwargs

: Kwargs passed to `np.loadtxt`, such as `skiprows`, `delimiter`.

# Hint

The data file should be in this format (`delimiter` and `skiprows` can be
customized in `**loadtxt_kwargs`):

- For lossless media:

  ```
  wl       n
  [float] [float]
  .       .
  .       .
  .       .
  ```

- For lossy media:

  ```
  wl       n       k
  [float] [float] [float]
  .       .       .
  .       .       .
  .       .       .
  ```

# Returns

{class}`DispersionFitter`

: A {class}`DispersionFitter` instance.

Tool for fitting refractive index data to get a
dispersive medium described by {class}`.PoleResidue` model.

documentation: Make sure `path_dir` exists and create one if not.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.container.Batch.run
documentation:
Upload and run each simulation in {class}`Batch`.

# Parameters

path_dir

: Base directory where data will be downloaded, by default current working directory.

# Returns

{class}`BatchData`

: Contains the {class}`.SimulationData` of each {class}`.Simulation` in {class}`Batch`.

# Note

A typical usage might look like:

```pycon
>>> batch_data = batch.run()
>>> for task_name, sim_data in batch_data.items():
...     # do something with data.
```

`bach_data` does not store all of the {class}`.SimulationData` objects in memory,
rather it iterates over the task names
and loads the corresponding {class}`.SimulationData` from file one by one.
If no file exists for that task, it downloads it.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.web.container.Batch.download
documentation:
Download results of each task.

# Parameters

path_dir

: Base directory where data will be downloaded, by default the current working directory.

# Note

To load the data into {class}`.SimulationData`objets, can call :meth:`Batch.items`.

The data for each task will be named as `{path_dir}/{task_name}.hdf5`.
The {class}`Batch` hdf5 file will be automatically saved as `{path_dir}/batch.hdf5`,
allowing one to load this {class}`Batch` later using `batch = Batch.from_file()`.

The {class}`Batch` hdf5 file will be automatically saved as `{path_dir}/batch.hdf5`,
allowing one to load this {class}`Batch` later using `batch = Batch.from_file()`.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.components.data.data_array.JaxDataArray.as_ndarray
documentation: ``self.values`` as a numpy array.
removed because it was slowing things down.
@pd.validator("coords", always=True)
def _coords_match_values(cls, val, values):
"""Make sure the coordinate dimensions and shapes match the values data."""
values = values.get("values")
# if values did not pass validation, just skip this validator
if values is None:
return None
# compute the shape, otherwise exit.
try:
shape = jnp.array(values).shape
except TypeError:
return val
if len(shape) != len(val):
raise AdjointError(f"'values' has '{len(shape)}' dims, but given '{len(val)}'.")
# make sure each coordinate list has same length as values along that axis
for len_dim, (coord_name, coord_list) in zip(shape, val.items()):
if len_dim != len(coord_list):
raise AdjointError(
f"coordinate '{coord_name}' has '{len(coord_list)}' elements, "
f"expected '{len_dim}' to match number of 'values' along this dimension."
return val

modules.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.transforms
documentation:
Coordinate transformations.

The Jacobian of a transformation from coordinates r = (x, y, z) into coordinates
r' = (u, v, w) is defined as J_ij = dr'_i/dr_j. Here, z and w are the propagation axes in the
original and transformed planes, respectively, and the coords are only provided in (x, y) and
transformed to (u, v). The Yee grid positions also have to be taken into account. The Jacobian
for the transformation of eps and E is evaluated at the r' positions of E-field components.
Similarly, the jacobian for mu and H is evaluated at the r' positions of H-field components.
Currently, the half-step offset in w is ignored, which should be a pretty good approximation.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.transforms.radial_transform
documentation:
Compute the new coordinates and the Jacobian of a polar coordinate transformation. After
offsetting the plane such that its center is a distance of `radius` away from the center of
curvature, we have, e.g. for `bend_axis=='y'`:

> u = (x\*\*2 + z\*\*2)
> v = y
> w = R acos(x / u)

These are all evaluated at z = 0 below.

# Parameters

coords

: A tuple of two arrays of size Nx + 1, Ny + 1, respectively.

radius

: Radius of the bend.

bend_axis

: Axis normal to the bend plane.

# Returns

new_coords: tuple

: Transformed coordinates, same shape as `coords`.

jac_e: np.ndarrray

: Jacobian of the transformation at the E-field positions, shape `(3, 3, Nx * Ny)`.

jac_h: np.ndarrray

: Jacobian of the transformation at the H-field positions, shape `(3, 3, Nx * Ny)`.

k_to_kp: np.ndarray

: A matrix of shape (3, 3) that transforms the k-vector from the original coordinates to the
  transformed ones.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.transforms.angled_transform
documentation:
Compute the new coordinates and the Jacobian for a transformation that "straightens"
an angled waveguide such that it is translationally invariant in w. The transformation is
u = x - tan(angle) * z

# Parameters

coords

: A tuple of two arrays of size Nx + 1, Ny + 1, respectively.

angle_theta

: (radian) Polar angle from the normal axis.

angle_phi

: (radian) Azimuth angle in the plane orthogonal to the normal axis.

# Returns

new_coords: tuple

: Transformed coordinates, same shape as `coords`.

jac_e: np.ndarrray

: Jacobian of the transformation at the E-field positions, shape `(3, 3, Nx * Ny)`.

jac_h: np.ndarrray

: Jacobian of the transformation at the H-field positions, shape `(3, 3, Nx * Ny)`.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.web.run
documentation:
Submits a {class}`.JaxSimulation` to server, starts running, monitors progress, downloads,
and loads results as a {class}`.JaxSimulationData` object.
Can be included within a function that will have `jax.grad` applied.

# Parameters

simulation

: Simulation to upload to server.

task_name

: Name of task.

path

: Path to download results file (.hdf5), including filename.

folder_name

: Name of folder to store task on web UI.

callback_url

: Http PUT url to receive simulation finish event. The body content is a json file with
  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

# Returns

{class}`.JaxSimulationData`

: Object containing solver results for the supplied {class}`.JaxSimulation`.
pylint:disable=too-many-arguments

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.web.run_fwd
documentation: Run forward pass and stash extra objects for the backwards pass.
pylint:disable=too-many-arguments

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.web.run_bwd
documentation: Run backward pass and return simulation storing vjp of the objective w.r.t. the sim.
pylint:disable=too-many-arguments

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.web._task_name_orig
documentation: Task name as function of index into simulations. Note: for original must be int.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.adjoint.web.run_async
documentation:
Submits a set of {class}`.JaxSimulation` objects to server, starts running,
monitors progress, downloads, and loads results
as a tuple of {class}`.JaxSimulationData` objects.
Uses `asyncio` to perform these steps asynchronously.

# Parameters

simulations

: Mapping of task name to simulation.

folder_name

: Name of folder to store each task on web UI.

path_dir

: Base directory where data will be downloaded, by default current working directory.

callback_url

: Http PUT url to receive simulation finish event. The body content is a json file with
  fields `{'id', 'status', 'name', 'workUnit', 'solverVersion'}`.

verbose

: If `True`, will print progressbars and status, otherwise, will run silently.

num_workers: int = None

: Number of tasks to submit at once in a batch, if None, will run all at the same time.

# Note

This is an experimental feature and may not work on all systems or configurations.
For more details, see `https://realpython.com/async-io-python/`.

# Returns

Tuple\[:class:JaxSimulationData, ...\]

: Contains the {class}`.JaxSimulationData` of each {class}`.JaxSimulation`.
pylint:disable=too-many-locals

modules.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver
documentation: Mode solver for propagating EM modes.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver.compute_modes
documentation:
Solve for the modes of a waveguide cross section.

# Parameters

eps_cross

: Either a single 2D array defining the relative permittivity in the cross-section, or three
  2D arrays defining the permittivity at the Ex, Ey, and Ez locations of the Yee grid.

coords

: Two 1D arrays with each with size one larger than the corresponding axis of `eps_cross`.
  Defines a (potentially non-uniform) Cartesian grid on which the modes are computed.

freq

: (Hertz) Frequency at which the eigenmodes are computed.

mode_spec

: `ModeSpec` object containing specifications of the mode solver.

# Returns

Tuple[Numpy, Numpy]

: The first array gives the E and H fields for all modes, the second one give sthe complex
  effective index.
pylint:disable=too-many-statements,too-many-branches,too-many-locals

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver.solver_em
documentation:
Solve for the electromagnetic modes of a system defined by in-plane permittivity and
permeability and assuming translational invariance in the normal direction.

Parameters
----------
Nx : int
    Number of grids along x-direction.
Ny : int
    Number of grids along y-direction.
eps_tensor : np.ndarray
    Shape (3, 3, N), the permittivity tensor at every point in the plane.
mu_tensor : np.ndarray
    Shape (3, 3, N), the permittivity tensor at every point in the plane.
der_mats : List[scipy.sparse.csr_matrix]
    The sparce derivative matrices dxf, dxb, dyf, dyb, including the PML.
num_modes : int
    Number of modes to solve for.
neff_guess : float
    Initial guess for the effective index.
mat_precision : Union['single', 'double']
    Single or double-point precision in eigensolver.

Returns
-------
E : np.ndarray
    Electric field of the eigenmodes, shape (3, N, num_modes).
H : np.ndarray
    Magnetic field of the eigenmodes, shape (3, N, num_modes).
neff : np.ndarray
    Real part of the effective index, shape (num_modes, ).
keff : np.ndarray
    Imaginary part of the effective index, shape (num_modes, ).

documentation: EM eigenmode solver assuming `eps` and `mu` are diagonal everywhere.

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver.solver_tensorial
documentation: EM eigenmode solver assuming `eps` or `mu` have off-diagonal elements.

Check if a numpy array or scipy csr_matrix has complex component by looking at

Parameters

vec_or_mat : Union[np.ndarray, sp.csr_matrix]

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver.type_conversion
documentation:
Convert vec_or_mat to new_type.

Parameters
----------
vec_or_mat : Union[np.ndarray, sp.csr_matrix]
    vec or mat to be converted.
new_dtype : Union[np.complex128, np.complex64, np.float64, np.float32]
    Final type of vec or mat

Returns
-------
converted_vec_or_mat : Union[np.ndarray, sp.csr_matrix]

functions.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.mode.solver.set_initial_vec
documentation:
Set initial vector for eigs:
1) The field at x=0 and y=0 boundaries are set to 0. This should be
the case for PEC boundaries, but wouldn't hurt for non-PEC boundary;
2) The vector is np.complex128 by default, and will be converted to
approriate type afterwards.

Parameters
----------
Nx : int
    Number of grids along x-direction.
Ny : int
    Number of grids along y-direction.
mat_dtype : Union[np.complex128, np.complex64, np.float64, np.float32]
    The type of returned vector.
is_tensorial : bool
    diagonal or tensorial eigenvalue problem.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab.from_gds
documentation:
Import {class}`.PolySlab` from a `gdstk.Cell`.

# Parameters

gds_cell

: `gdstk.Cell` containing 2D geometric data.

axis

: Integer index into the polygon's slab axis. (0,1,2) -> (x,y,z).

slab_bounds: Tuple[float, float]

: Minimum and maximum positions of the slab along `axis`.

gds_layer

: Layer index in the `gds_cell`.

gds_dtype

: Data-type index in the `gds_cell`.
  If `None`, imports all data for this layer into the returned list.

gds_scale

: Length scale used in GDS file in units of MICROMETER.
  For example, if gds file uses nanometers, set `gds_scale=1e-3`.
  Must be positive.

dilation

: Dilation of the polygon in the base by shifting each edge along its
  normal outwards direction by a distance;
  a negative value corresponds to erosion.

sidewall_angle

: Angle of the sidewall.
  `sidewall_angle=0` (default) specifies vertical wall,
  while `0<sidewall_angle<np.pi/2` for the base to be larger than the top.

reference_plane

: The position of the GDS layer. It can be at the `bottom`, `middle`,
  or `top` of the PolySlab. E.g. if `axis=1`, `bottom` refers to the
  negative side of y-axis, and `top` refers to the positive side of y-axis.

# Returns

List\[{class}`.PolySlab`\]

: List of {class}`.PolySlab` objects sharing `axis` and slab bound properties.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab.to_structure
documentation:
Construct a structure containing a user-specified medium
and a GeometryGroup made of all the divided PolySlabs from this object.

# Parameters

medium

: Medium for the complex polyslab.

# Returns

{class}`.Structure`

: The structure containing all divided polyslabs made of a user-specified
  medium.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab.geometry_group
documentation:
Divide a complex polyslab into a list of simple polyslabs, which
are assembled into a {class}`.GeometryGroup`.

: GeometryGroup for a list of simple polyslabs divided from the complex

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab.sub_polyslabs
documentation:
Divide a complex polyslab into a list of simple polyslabs.
Only neighboring vertex-vertex crossing events are treated in this
version.

Returns
-------
List[PolySlab]
    A list of simple polyslabs.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab._dilation_length
documentation: dilation length from reference plane to the top/bottom of the polyslab.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.polyslab.polyslab.ComplexPolySlab._dilation_value_at_reference_to_coord
documentation: Compute the coordinate based on the dilation value to the reference plane.

Interface for dividing a complex polyslab where self-intersecting polygon can
occur during extrusion.

# Example

```pycon
>>> vertices = ((0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1), (0, 0.9), (0, 0.11))
>>> p = ComplexPolySlab(vertices=vertices, axis=2, slab_bounds=(0, 1), sidewall_angle=0.785)
>>> # To obtain the divided polyslabs, there are two approaches:
>>> # 1) a list of divided polyslabs
>>> geo_list = p.sub_polyslabs
>>> # 2) geometry group containing the divided polyslabs
>>> geo_group = p.geometry_group
>>> # Or directly obtain the structure with a user-specified medium
>>> mat = td.Medium(permittivity=2)
>>> structure = p.to_structure(mat)
```

# Note

This version is limited to neighboring vertex-vertex crossing type of
self-intersecting events. Extension to cover all types of self-intersecting
events is expected in the future.

The algorithm is as follows (for the convenience of illustration,
let's consider the reference plane to lie at the bottom of the polyslab),

1\. Starting from the reference plane, find out the critical
extrusion distance for the first vertices degeneracy
event when marching towards the top of the polyslab;

2\. Construct a sub-polyslab whose base is the polygon at
the reference plane and height to be the critical
extrusion distance;

3\. At the critical extrusion distance, constructing a new polygon
that keeps only one of the degenerate vertices;

4\. Set the reference plane to the position of the new polygon,
and repeating 1-3 to construct sub-polyslabs until reaching
the top of the polyslab, or all vertices collapsed into a 1D curve
or a 0D point.

documentation:
Return a copy of the {class}`.ModeSolverData` with the fields renormalized to account
for propagation on a finite grid along the propagation direction. The fields are assumed to
have `E exp(1j k r)` dependence on the finite grid and are then resampled using linear
interpolation to the exact position of the mode plane. This is needed to correctly compute
overlap with fields that come from a {class}`.FieldMonitor` placed in the same grid.

grid

: Numerical grid on which the modes are assumed to propagate.

# Returns

{class}`.ModeSolverData`

: Copy of the data with renormalized fields.

Plot the field for a {class}`.ModeSolverData` with {class}`.Simulation` plot overlayed.

# Parameters

field_name

: Name of `field` component to plot (eg. `'Ex'`).
  Also accepts `'int'` to plot intensity.

val

: Which part of the field to plot.
  If `field_name == 'int'`, this has no effect.

eps_alpha

: Opacity of the structure permittivity.
  Must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive).

robust

: If True and vmin or vmax are absent, uses the 2nd and 98th percentiles of the data
  to compute the color limits. This helps in visualizing the field patterns especially
  in the presence of a source.

vmin

: The lower bound of data range that the colormap covers. If `None`, they are
  inferred from the data and other keyword arguments.

vmax

: The upper bound of data range that the colormap covers. If `None`, they are
  inferred from the data and other keyword arguments.

ax

: matplotlib axes to plot on, if not specified, one is created.

sel_kwargs

: These kwargs can select over the spatial dimensions (`x`, `y`, `z`),
  frequency or time dimensions (`f`, `t`) or `mode_index`, if applicable.
  For the plotting to work appropriately, the resulting data after selection must contain
  only two coordinates with len > 1.
  Furthermore, these should be spatial coordinates (`x`, `y`, or `z`).

# Returns

matplotlib.axes.\_subplots.Axes

: The supplied or created matplotlib axes.

Interface for solving electromagnetic eigenmodes in a 2D plane with translational
invariance in the third dimension.

classes.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder

documentation: Validate ``freq_window``

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder.run
documentation:
Finds resonances in a {class}`.FieldTimeData` or a Tuple of such.
The time coordinates must be uniformly spaced, and the spacing must be the same
across all supplied data. The resonance finder runs on the sum of the
`Ex`, `Ey`, and `Ez` fields of all the supplied data,
unless no electric fields are present at all, in which case it runs on the sum of the
`Hx`, `Hy`, and `Hz` fields.
Note that the signal should start after the sources have turned off.

# Parameters

signals: {class}`.FieldTimeData`

: Data to search for resonances

# Returns

xr.Dataset

: Dataset containing the decay rate, Q, amplitude, phase, and estimation error
  of the resonances as a function of frequency. Modes with low Q, small
  amplitude, or high estimation error are likely to be spurious.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder.run_scalar_field_time
documentation:
Finds resonances in a {class}`.ScalarFieldTimeDataArray`.
The time coordinates must be uniformly spaced to use the resonance finder;
the time step is calculated automatically.
Note that the signal should start after the sources have turned off.

# Parameters

signal

: Time series to search for resonances

# Returns

xr.Dataset

: Dataset containing the decay rate, Q, amplitude, phase, and estimation error
  of the resonances as a function of frequency. Modes with low Q, small
  amplitude, or high estimation error are likely to be spurious.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder.run_raw_signal
documentation:
Finds resonances in a time series.
Note that the signal should start after the sources have turned off.

Parameters
----------
signal : List[complex]

    to search for resonances.
time_step : float

Returns

    of the resonances as a function of frequency. Modes with low Q, small
    amplitude, or high estimation error are likely to be spurious.

documentation: Perform the Gram-Schmidt process on the columns of a matrix.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder._solve_gen_eig_prob
documentation:
Solve a generalized eigenvalue problem of the form

$$$$

Av = lambda B v

We make B nonsingular by eliminating eigenvalues smaller
than `` `rcond `` times the largest eigenvalue. This method is used in
Michael R. Wall and Daniel Neuhauser,
"Extraction, through filter-diagonalization, of general quantum eigenvalues
or classical normal mode frequencies of a short-time segment of a signal.
I. Theory and application to a quantum-dynamics model,"
J. Chem. Phys. 102, 8001 (1995).

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder._find_amplitudes
documentation: Compute the resonance amplitudes.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder._find_errors
documentation: Estimate the eigenvalue error.

methods.name: SiEPIC.tidy3d.tidy3d.plugins.resonance.resonance.ResonanceFinder._iterate
documentation: Run a single iteration of the resonance finder.

Tool that extracts resonance information from a time series of the form shown below.
The resonance information consists of frequency $f$, decay rate $\alpha$,
Q factor $Q = \pi |f|/\alpha$, amplitude $a$, and phase $\phi$.

# Note

$$
f(t) = \sum_k a_k e^{i \phi_k} e^{-2 \pi i f_k t - \alpha_k t}
$$

# Note

We use the algorithm described in

Vladimir A. Mandelshtam and Howard S. Taylor,
"Harmonic inversion of time signals and its applications,"
J. Chem. Phys. 107, 6756 (1997).

# Example

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from tidy3d.plugins import ResonanceFinder
>>> t = np.linspace(0, 10000, 10000)
>>> f1 = 2*np.pi*0.1 - 1j*0.002
>>> f2 = 2*np.pi*0.2 - 1j*0.0005
>>> sig = 2*np.exp(-1j*f1*t) + 3*1j*np.exp(-1j*f2*t)
>>> resfinder = ResonanceFinder(freq_window=(0.05, 0.25))
>>> resdata = resfinder.run_raw_signal(signal=sig, time_step=1)
>>> resdata.to_dataframe()
```

modules.name: SiEPIC.utils.sampling
documentation: Adapted from: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14084634/adaptive-plotting-of-a-function-in-python
Old license: "Adaptive sampling of 1D functions" by unknown CC0
Modified by Thomas Ferreira de Lima @thomaslima
License: CC BY 4.0 https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.sampling.sample_function
documentation:
Sample a 1D function to given tolerance by adaptive subdivision.

The function itself can be multidimensional.

The result of sampling is a set of points that, if plotted,
produces a smooth curve with also sharp features of the function
resolved.

# Parameters

func

: Function func(x) of a single argument. It is assumed to be vectorized.

points

: Initial points to sample, sorted in ascending order.
  These will determine also the bounds of sampling.

tol

: Tolerance to sample to. The condition is roughly that the total
  length of the curve on the (x, y) plane is computed up to this
  tolerance.

min_point

: Minimum number of points to sample.

max_level

: Maximum subdivision depth.

sample_transform

: Function w = g(x, y). The x-samples are generated so that w
  is sampled.

# Returns

x

: X-coordinates

y

: Corresponding values of func(x)

# Notes

This routine is useful in computing functions that are expensive
to compute, and have sharp features --- it makes more sense to
adaptively dedicate more sampling points for the sharp features
than the smooth parts.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> def func(x):
...     '''Function with a sharp peak on a smooth background'''
...     a = 0.001
...     return x + a**2/(a**2 + x**2)
...
>>> x, y = sample_function(func, [-1, 1], tol=1e-3)
```

```pycon
>>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>>> xx = np.linspace(-1, 1, 12000)
>>> plt.plot(xx, func(xx), '-', x, y[0], '.')
>>> plt.show()
```

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.crossings.insert_crossing

# Inserts a crossing at the crossed locations between pairs of paths and returns the a selection to the newly created paths

# the process is n^2 to the number of paths selected, which increase by two everytime a path is found and restarts, making it

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.geometry.bezier_cubic
documentation:
Calculate a cubic Bezier curve between Points P0 and P3, 
where the control point positions P1 and P2 are determined by
the angles at P0 (angle0) and P3 (angle3), 
at a distance of a * scale from P0, and b * scale from P3,
where scale is the longest segment in a Manhattan route between P0 and P3.

Args:
    P0, P3: pya.DPoint (in microns)
    angle0, angle3: radians
    a, b: <float>. 0 corresponds to P1=P0, and 1 corresponds to P1 at the corner of a 90º bend
    accuracy: 0.001 = 1 nm

Returns:
    list of pya.DPoint

Example:
    Bezier curve can approximate a 1/4 circle (arc) for a=b=0.553
        # https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1734745/how-to-create-circle-with-b%C3%A9zier-curves

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.geometry.translate_from_normal2
documentation:
Translate each point in the array pts, by its local normal by a distance that 
 varies linearly starting at 'trans' and ending 'trans2' 
Useful for making an S-bend with different waveguide widths at the ends

Author: Lukas Chrostowski

Inputs:
 pts: DPoint array, length at least 3
 trans: float
 trans2: float
 
Output:
 tpts: DPoint array
 
Example, SBend Waveguide with variable width:

from SiEPIC.utils.geometry import bezier_parallel

w1 = 0.15 # input waveguide width
wo = 0.35 # output waveguide width
l = 5     # horizontal length
offset = 2# vertical offset

p = bezier_parallel(DPoint(0,0), DPoint(l,offset), 0)
pt1 = translate_from_normal2(p,w1/2,wo/2)
pt2 = translate_from_normal2(p,-w1/2, -wo/2)
pt = pt1+pt2[::-1]

from SiEPIC.utils import get_layout_variables
TECHNOLOGY, lv, ly, cell = get_layout_variables()
layer = cell.layout().layer(TECHNOLOGY['Waveguide'])
poly = pya.DPolygon(pt)
cell.shapes(layer).insert(poly)
Curve translation:

Input width, height: in microns
Return a pya.DPolygon of a box with rounded corners, using an optimal Bezier curve
with the control points being a fraction (dt_bezier_corner from 0 to 1) away from each corner
accuracy 0.001 is 1 nm

Some call them squircles: https://arun.is/blog/apple-rounded-corners/

modules.name: SiEPIC.install
documentation:
Installing Python packages inside KLayout is sometimes possibles, and SiEPIC tools requires some packages
that don't come preinstalled

The preferred function is "install" which is simple to use

Usage:

# Required packages
from SiEPIC.install import install
if not install('scipy', requested_by='Contra Directional Coupler design'):
  pya.MessageBox.warning(
  "Missing package", "The simulator does not function without the package 'scipy'.",  pya.MessageBox.Ok)    


by Lukas Chrostowski, 2023

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.find_automated_measurement_labels
documentation:
return all opt_in labels from a cell
requires a layout with Text labels on the layer LayerTextN
the format of the labels is
   opt_in_<polarization>_<wavelength>_<type>_<deviceID>_<params>
     or 
   opt_<polarization>_<wavelength>_<type>_<deviceID>_<params>
     or
   elec_<deviceID>_<params>
     or
   pwb_<recipeID>_<params>
for electrical-optical measurements, the deviceID on the electrical contact
needs to match that of the optical input
   
returns:
    text_out: HTML text
    opt_in: a Dictionary

example usage:
topcell = pya.Application.instance().main_window().current_view().active_cellview().cell
LayerText = pya.LayerInfo(10, 0)
LayerTextN = topcell.layout().layer(LayerText)
find_automated_measurement_labels(topcell, LayerTextN)

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_waveguide3
documentation:
Create a waveguide, in a specific technology
inputs
- cell: into which Cell we add the waveguide
    from SiEPIC.utils import get_layout_variables
    TECHNOLOGY, lv, layout, cell = get_layout_variables()
- pts: a list of pya.Points, in database units (e.g., nm)
- params, obtained from load_Waveguides_by_Tech and Waveguides.XML
    must be a primitive waveguide type containing <component> info
output:
- waveguide
- DevRec, PinRec
by Lukas Chrostowski

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_waveguide2
documentation:
Create a waveguide, in a specific technology
inputs
- TECHNOLOGY, layout, cell:
    from SiEPIC.utils import get_layout_variables
    TECHNOLOGY, lv, layout, cell = get_layout_variables()
- layers: list of text names, e.g., ['Waveguide']
- widths: list of floats in units Microns, e.g., [0.50]
- offsets: list of floats in units Microns, e.g., [0]
- pts: a list of pya.Points, in database units (e.g., nm)
    e.g. 
    L=15/dbu
    pts = [Point(0,0), Point(L,0), Point(L,L)]
- radius: in Microns, e.g., 5
- adiab: 1 = Bezier curve, 0 = radial bend (arc)
- bezier: the bezier parameter, between 0 and 0.45 (almost a radial bend)
- sbends (optional): sbends (Boolean)
Note: bezier parameters need to be simulated and optimized, and will depend on 
    wavelength, polarization, width, etc.  TM and rib waveguides don't benefit from bezier curves
    most useful for TE 
by Lukas Chrostowski

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_waveguide
documentation:
Lays out a waveguide (or trace) with a certain width with along given points.

This is very useful for laying out Bezier curves with or without adiabatic tapers.

Args:
    cell: cell to place into
    layer: layer to place into. It is done with cell.shapes(layer).insert(pya.Polygon)
    points_list: list of pya.DPoint (at least 2 points)
    width (microns): constant or list. If list, then it has to have the same length as points

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_ring
documentation:
Produce the layout of a ring

Args:
    cell: layout cell to place the layout
    layer: layer index to use, cell.layout.layer()
    center: origin pya.DPoint
    r: radius, units in microns
    w: waveguide width, units in microns

Example usage:  
Places the ring layout in the presently selected cell.
cell = pya.Application.instance().main_window().current_view().active_cellview().cell
layout_ring(cell, cell.layout().layer(LayerInfo(1, 0)), pya.DPoint(0,0), 10, 0.5)

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_square

Lays out a taper

Args:

    w2: width of waveguide, float for DPoint type (microns); int for Point type (nm)
    length: length, float
    insert: flag to insert drawn waveguide or return shape, boolean

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.layout_waveguide_sbend_bezier
documentation:
Creates a waveguide s-bend using a bezier curve
Author: Lukas Chrostowski
Args:
    trans: pya.Trans: location and rotation
    w: width of input waveguide, float for DPoint type (microns); int for Point type (nm)
    wo (optional): width of output waveguide, float
    h: height
    length: length
    insert: flag to insert drawn waveguide or return shape, boolean
Usage:
    from SiEPIC.utils import get_layout_variables
    TECHNOLOGY, lv, ly, cell = get_layout_variables()
    layer = cell.layout().layer(TECHNOLOGY['Waveguide'])
    layout_waveguide_sbend_bezier(cell, layer, pya.Trans(), w=0.5, h=2.0, length=15.0, insert = True)

functions.name: SiEPIC.utils.layout.y_splitter_tree
documentation:
Create a tree of splitters
- cell: layout cell to create the structures in
- tree_depth: Tree depth (2^N outputs)
- y_splitter_cell: name of the y-splitter cell
- library: the library containing the y_splitter_cell

Returns

- inst_out[]: array of instances of the output cells

This is useful for subsequent routing

SBends:
- the design uses either regular 90 degree bends, or S-bends if the Waveguides.xml allows for it

modules.name: SiEPIC.extend
documentation:
This module extends several pya classes that are useful for the library.

pya.Layout:
  - get_technology: get the technology for the specific layout
  - load_Waveguide_types: load the waveguide types from WAVEGUIDES.XML
  - cell_character_replacement: replace forbidden characters
  
dbu float-int extension:
  - to_dbu and to_itype, convert float (microns) to integer (nanometers) using dbu
  - from_dbu and to_dtype, convert integer (nanometers) to float (microns) using dbu

pya.Path and pya.DPath Extensions:
  - get_points(), returns list of pya.Points
  - get_dpoints(), returns list of pya.DPoints
  - is_manhattan(), tests to see if the path is manhattan
  - is_manhattan_endsegments(), tests to see if the path is manhattan (only the 1st and last segments)
  - radius_check(radius), tests to see of all path segments are long enough to be
    converted to a waveguide with bends of radius 'radius'
  - remove_colinear_points(), removes all colinear points in place
  - unique_points(), remove all but one colinear points
  - translate_from_center(offset), returns a new path whose points have been offset
    by 'offset' from the center of the original path
  - snap(pins), snaps the path in place to the nearest pin
  - snap_m, snaps the path to the nearest metal pins [Karl McNulty, RIT Integrated Photonics Group]
  - to_dtype(dbu), for KLayout < 0.25, integer to float using dbu
  - to_itype(dbu), for KLayout < 0.25, float to integer using dbu

pya.Polygon and pya.DPolygon Extensions:
  - get_points(), returns list of pya.Points
  - get_dpoints(), returns list of pya.DPoints
  - to_dtype(dbu), for KLayout < 0.25, integer using dbu to float

pya.PCellDeclarationHelper Extensions:
  - print_parameter_list, prints parameter list

pya.Cell Extensions:
  - print_parameter_values, if this cell is a pcell, prints the parameter values
  - find_pins: find Pin object of either the specified name or all pins in a cell
  - find_pin
  - find_pins_component
  - find_components
  - identify_nets
  - get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers
  - get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers_from_opt_in
  - spice_netlist_export
  - check_component_models
  - pinPoint
  - plot: display an image for Jupyter notebook

pya.Instance Extensions:
  - find_pins: find Pin objects for all pins in a cell instance
  - pinPoint

pya.Point Extensions:
  - to_dtype(dbu): for KLayout < 0.25, convert integer Point using dbu to float DPoint
  - angle_vector
  - to_p(): for KLayout < 0.25
SiEPIC Class Extension of KLayout PYA Library                  #

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.find_pins
documentation:
Find Pins in a Cell.
Inputs:
    self: pya.Cell
    verbose: True prints details for debugging
    polygon_devrec:

Optical Pins have:
 1) path on layer PinRec, indicating direction (out of component)
 2) text on layer PinRec, inside the path
Electrical Pins have:
 1) path on layer PinRecM, indicating direction (out of component)
 2) text on layer PinRecM, inside the path

Old version:
Electrical Pins have:
 1) box on layer PinRec
 2) text on layer PinRec, inside the box
 
Returns:
    pins: SiEPIC.core.Pin
    pin_errors: text

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.find_pins_component
documentation: find the pins inside a component

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.find_components
documentation:
Function to traverse the cell's hierarchy and find all the components
returns list of components (class Component)
Use the DevRec shapes.  
Assumption: One DevRec shape per component.  We return a component "Flattened" if more than one DevRec is found

Find all the DevRec shapes; identify the component it belongs; record the info as a Component
for each component instance, also find the Pins and Fibre ports.

Find all the pins for the component, save in components and also return pin list.
Use the pin names on layer PinRec to sort the pins in alphabetical order

cell_selected: only find components that match this specific cell.

inst: return only the component that matches the instance inst

raiseException: False turns of exception handling, and returns None instead

limitation:
 - flat components only. doesn't find the component if it is buried in a hierarchy
 - no function for instance.find_components.  Instead we find based on cell, then try to match it to the requested instance.

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.identify_nets
documentation: end def find_components

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers
documentation:
Find - LumericalINTERCONNECT_Laser
     - LumericalINTERCONNECT_Detector
get their parameters
determine which OpticalIO they are connected to, and find their nets
Assume that the detectors and laser are on the topcell (not subcells); don't perform transformations.

returns: parameters, nets in order

usage:
laser_net, detector_nets, wavelength_start, wavelength_stop, wavelength_points, ignoreOpticalIOs = get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers(topcell, components)

functions.name: SiEPIC.extend.get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers_from_opt_in
documentation:
From the opt_in label, find the trimmed circuit, and assign a laser and detectors

returns: parameters, nets in order

usage:
laser_net, detector_nets, wavelength_start, wavelength_stop, wavelength_points, ignoreOpticalIOs, detector_list = get_LumericalINTERCONNECT_analyzers_from_opt_in(topcell, components)
